title: Living in Boulder vlog 5 - 5 - 2017: Caesar, Cicero & Gibbon prepping to put on site

enrichment.summary: Kyle recaps a day lost to an overfull computer and an evening at his mom's, then describes rapid progress adding ancient Roman and Greek primary sources to his website. He explains his preference for full single-page books (easier to read continuously, supports word counts, and links to his timeline) and lists upcoming additions including Polybius, Strabo, Sallust's Catiline Conspiracy, and Frontinus on aqueducts.
enrichment.speaker: kyle

stances.position: strongly prefers full books on one page over Wikipedia-style multi-page splits for readability, word counts, and timeline linking
wants to put all available ancient historical primary sources on his site; finds the process increases his understanding of the texts

transcript.text: Hello, Living in Boulder V-Log for May 5th. I didn't do one yesterday or the day before because I was just too pressured.
The day before yesterday was kind of a lost, wasted day because I was fighting my computer all day long.
My computer was fighting Livy all day long because it's just too big.
I added more stuff to Livy and it was almost too big before and now it was almost unmanageable, but I managed to do it.
It took all day and then I went over to my mom's house and she made me an omelet and a salad.
Then I came back and I finally started on doing more books on my website and with this new fast computer I can do really fast.
I did, I think in the first night I almost completed Annals and then yesterday I did Tacitus Annals Histories
Because I want to do all of the history in Rome to connect it to my timeline, the horizontal part of the timeline.
So you can click it and it takes you to that chapter of the book.
And so, yeah, so that would be Annals Histories and then, I did Julius Caesar and now I'm like a third of the way through Decline and Fall, the Roman Empire.
I want to, you know, I mean, I'm going by pretty quickly so I think I'm going to go ahead and do all the good stuff
Because the most, from ancient history, all of the direct historical material, there's not that much of it.
Like, I could get it all on my website in like a week or something.
I think I want to do, after this, I want to do, um, uh, um, I want to do Polybius because he talks about the Punic War.
I want to do Strabo because he talks about geography and he's really smart.
Um, and, oh yeah, the Catiline Conspiracy.
That's another historical tale written from back in the day.
And then I want to do, um, Frontinus, um, Aqueducts.
And then the guy who does, about, talks about, um, the war, the army.
And then, um, there's this other dude I'm listening to now from 150.
Oh yeah, and then I think St. Augustine and then, uh, Thucydides.
Yeah, I might just go buck wild.
So we'll see what I can do.
So I'll just ride this wave as long as I'm, like, tripping out over how cool it is to put all these books on my site.
Because I want to see it.
It's a bit, it increases my understanding a lot by putting a book on my site than, like, in Wikisource.
Because, uh, the way everybody else has it is it's all just, uh, broken up into lots of pages.
I like to have it all on one page.
Because then you can read, you can have the whole thing read to you all day long without having to read, click it.
And also, you can do word counts.
And you can find out exactly how big the book is.
I want to, I need to know how big the books are.
And then how big the chapters are.
That's the first step of understanding the book.
And then you, you know, find out when they're talking.
So, with the timeline.
So, um, that's what I'm doing now.
Um, ooh, 3.35, I think I've talked long enough.
Yeah, I'll try to do video every day.
But, yeah, I just, I suddenly got going on this.
Ooh, yeah, yeah, it's riveting.
Um, alright, so I'm going to get going on to climb the fall now.
Peace out.
